dyed_lifted_polyps: Endoscopic image showing polyps that have been lifted and stained with dye to enhance visualization.
Dyed lifted polyps appear as raised lesions with enhanced contrast due to the application of chromoendoscopy.
The dye highlights the borders and surface patterns of the polyps, aiding in their identification and assessment.
These images are typically used to evaluate the morphology and resectability of colorectal polyps.
The lifted appearance is due to submucosal injection, making the polyp more prominent against the surrounding mucosa.
dyed_resection_margins: Endoscopic image of resection margins stained with dye to assess completeness of polyp removal.
The dyed resection margins help visualize any residual tissue or incomplete excision after polypectomy.
Chromoendoscopy enhances the contrast between normal mucosa and the resection site.
These images are important for ensuring that no neoplastic tissue remains at the margins.
The dye application allows for clear delineation of the resection area and detection of subtle abnormalities.
esophagitis: Endoscopic image showing inflammation of the esophageal mucosa, characteristic of esophagitis.
Esophagitis appears as erythema, erosions, or ulcerations in the lining of the esophagus.
The mucosa may be swollen, friable, and may bleed easily upon contact.
These findings are often associated with gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) or infection.
The image may show longitudinal furrows, white exudates, or mucosal breaks depending on severity.
normal_cecum: Endoscopic image of a normal cecum, showing healthy mucosa and typical anatomical landmarks.
The cecum appears as a smooth, glistening surface with no evidence of inflammation, polyps, or lesions.
Normal cecal images display the ileocecal valve and appendiceal orifice without abnormal findings.
There are no signs of bleeding, ulceration, or abnormal growths in the normal cecum.
This image serves as a reference for comparison with pathological findings in the cecum.
normal_pylorus: Endoscopic image of a normal pylorus, the muscular opening between the stomach and duodenum.
The pylorus appears as a round, symmetric structure with smooth mucosal lining and no abnormalities.
There is no evidence of ulceration, mass, or narrowing in the normal pyloric region.
The image shows a healthy transition from the gastric antrum to the duodenum.
A normal pylorus allows for unobstructed passage of gastric contents into the small intestine.
normal_z_line: Endoscopic image of the normal Z-line, the squamocolumnar junction at the distal esophagus.
The Z-line appears as a sharp, well-defined boundary between the pale esophageal squamous mucosa and the reddish gastric columnar mucosa.
There are no signs of irregularity, inflammation, or metaplasia at the normal Z-line.
This anatomical landmark is important for identifying the gastroesophageal junction.
A normal Z-line is smooth and continuous, without nodularity or erosions.
polyps: Endoscopic image showing one or more polyps, which are abnormal growths protruding from the mucosal surface.
Polyps may appear as sessile or pedunculated lesions with variable size and shape.
The surface of polyps can be smooth or lobulated, and they may be single or multiple.
Polyps are important to identify as some may have malignant potential and require removal.
The image highlights the distinct appearance of polyps compared to the surrounding normal mucosa.
ulcerative_colitis: Endoscopic image showing features of ulcerative colitis, a chronic inflammatory disease of the colon.
Ulcerative colitis is characterized by continuous mucosal inflammation, erythema, and ulceration.
The mucosa may appear friable, with loss of vascular pattern and spontaneous bleeding.
Pseudopolyps and superficial ulcerations are common findings in active ulcerative colitis.
The image demonstrates diffuse involvement of the colon, distinguishing it from segmental diseases.
